message.content:
You are confirmer #2, independent. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (fully closes it; for a code change the named regression test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if the fix were reverted). Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix G-014 in tests/test_observability.py
What: test-discrimination: SIGTERM flush handler asserted unconditionally on the main thread.
Test(s): test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm
Steps: read the actual changed file(s); read the test and judge whether it discriminates; check no regression. For a doc/refactor item, judge correctness/coverage rather than revert-discrimination. Verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN (something missing/wrong/under-tested). Be skeptical and specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Show the diff for the test file in the fix commit

content.content:
commit 7dccfd8b5f8563c0da90d72ee92d954b99605422
Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Wed Jun 17 00:10:40 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p4): batch E — egress, error contracts, PII & test discrimination (8 findings)
    
    Pass-4 confirmed findings (2-agent verified, revert-discriminated):
    
    - G-007 (medium) the transcript scrubber redacts the bare operator username everywhere
      (git author strings, project-dir slugs, prose), not only inside /Users|/home paths.
    - G-018 (low) the high-stakes egress denylist now scrubs the ENTIRE ANTHROPIC_* namespace
      (any endpoint/auth/header/custom-header var) plus NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS /
      NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED (custom-CA / cert-bypass MITM enablers).
    - G-019 (low) Gate.evaluate type-guards the precondition's return (non-GateDecision ->
      typed GateError), the gate analogue of the #F-021 Verdict guards.
    - G-020 (low) make_gate_node records to the trail before returning the state update and, on
      a recorder failure, raises a clear GateError instead of returning the update — so graph
      state can't diverge from the tamper-evident trail.
    - G-021 (low) build_baseline_artifact names the offending extra_input key when it isn't
      canonicalizable (was a raw, context-free ValueError/TypeError from hash_value).
    - G-024 (low) corrected the cosine_similarity_matrix_array docstring: the determinism freeze
      copies the array bytes once (O(size)), it is NOT zero-copy/O(1).
    - G-013 (medium) test-discrimination: the cosine fast-path test now asserts dispatch to the
      numpy path AND numpy==pure agreement (a revert to always-pure now fails).
    - G-014 (medium) test-discrimination: the SIGTERM flush test asserts the handler is installed
      unconditionally on the main thread (was gated on `if "h" in captured`, silently skippable).
    
    Gates: ruff + ruff format --check + mypy --strict clean; 520 passed, 5 skipped.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

diff --git a/tests/test_observability.py b/tests/test_observability.py
index c9c4334..3ffa645 100644
--- a/tests/test_observability.py
+++ b/tests/test_observability.py
@@ -182,12 +182,20 @@ def test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm(monkeypatch):
 
     tr._install_flush_on_exit(_Provider())
 
+    import threading
+
     assert calls["atexit"], "atexit flush handler not registered"
     calls["atexit"][0]()  # simulate interpreter exit
     assert calls["flush"] >= 1
-    if "h" in captured:  # SIGTERM handler installed (main thread)
+    # #G-014: the SIGTERM assertion was gated on `if "h" in captured`, so removing the SIGTERM
+    # wiring silently skipped it. pytest runs on the main thread, where the handler MUST be
+    # installed — assert it unconditionally there so a regression that drops the wiring fails.
+    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
+        assert "h" in captured, "SIGTERM flush handler not installed on the main thread (#G-014)"
         captured["h"](_signal.SIGTERM, None)
         assert calls["shutdown"] >= 1
+    else:  # pragma: no cover - the suite runs on the main thread
+        pass
 
 
 def test_install_flush_on_exit_noop_without_force_flush(monkeypatch):

162	def test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm(monkeypatch):
163	    # span-flush on exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM must be wired and call force_flush/shutdown,
164	    # else buffered provenance spans are lost on container stop. No backend needed.
165	    import signal as _signal
166	
167	    calls = {"flush": 0, "shutdown": 0, "atexit": []}
168	
169	    class _Provider:
170	        def force_flush(self):
171	            calls["flush"] += 1
172	
173	        def shutdown(self):
174	            calls["shutdown"] += 1
175	
176	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.atexit, "register", lambda fn: calls["atexit"].append(fn))
177	    captured = {}
178	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.signal, "getsignal", lambda s: _signal.SIG_DFL)
179	    monkeypatch.setattr(
180	        tr.signal, "signal", lambda sig, handler: captured.__setitem__("h", handler)
181	    )
182	
183	    tr._install_flush_on_exit(_Provider())
184	
185	    import threading
186	
187	    assert calls["atexit"], "atexit flush handler not registered"
188	    calls["atexit"][0]()  # simulate interpreter exit
189	    assert calls["flush"] >= 1
190	    # #G-014: the SIGTERM assertion was gated on `if "h" in captured`, so removing the SIGTERM
191	    # wiring silently skipped it. pytest runs on the main thread, where the handler MUST be
192	    # installed — assert it unconditionally there so a regression that drops the wiring fails.
193	    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
194	        assert "h" in captured, "SIGTERM flush handler not installed on the main thread (#G-014)"
195	        captured["h"](_signal.SIGTERM, None)
196	        assert calls["shutdown"] >= 1
197	    else:  # pragma: no cover - the suite runs on the main thread
198	        pass
199	
200	
201	def test_install_flush_on_exit_noop_without_force_flush(monkeypatch):
202	    # a provider lacking force_flush must not register anything (no crash)
203	    calls = []
204	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.atexit, "register", lambda fn: calls.append(fn))
205	    tr._install_flush_on_exit(object())
206	    assert calls == []
207	
208	
209	# ---- experiment tracking ----

import re
p="src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py"
s=open(p).read()
# Remove the SIGTERM main-thread block by replacing the whole signal install with a pass
block='''    atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))
    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
        try:
            prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)

            def _handler(signum: int, frame: Any) -> None:
                _safe(flush)
                _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
                if callable(prior):
                    prior(signum, frame)

            signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
        except (ValueError, OSError):  # pragma: no cover - not on main thread / unsupported
            pass'''
assert block in s
s=s.replace(block,"    atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))")
open(p,"w").write(s)
print("reverted")
EOF

content.text:
Source is restored (no diff), full observability suite passes. 

Let me confirm the pre-fix behavior: the old `if "h" in captured:` form would silently skip the SIGTERM assertion entirely if the handler weren't installed — which is exactly the under-discrimination flaw. The diff shows the old line was `if "h" in captured:` (gated/skippable), now replaced with an unconditional `assert "h" in captured` on the main thread. Verified.

Analysis complete:

- **Appropriate**: The fix replaces a self-gating conditional (`if "h" in captured:` — which silently skips when the handler is absent, the textbook under-discrimination anti-pattern) with `assert "h" in captured` guarded only by `threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread()`. This guard correctly mirrors the source's own main-thread gate in `_install_flush_on_exit` (tracing.py:131), so the test is precise rather than over-asserting on non-main-thread runners. The `else: pass` branch is dead-but-honest (`# pragma: no cover`). Idiomatic, no regression — full suite 23 passed / 2 skipped.
- **Complete / discriminates**: I reverted the SIGTERM wiring in the source and the test failed with `AssertionError: SIGTERM flush handler not installed on the main thread (#G-014)`. Restored, it passes. The named test fails if the fix is reverted.

input.reasoning:
G-014 was a test-discrimination finding: the SIGTERM flush assertion in test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm was gated on `if "h" in captured:`, so removing the SIGTERM wiring would silently skip the assertion (self-gating anti-pattern). The fix (commit 7dccfd8, tests/test_observability.py:185-198) replaces the self-gating conditional with `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): assert "h" in captured, "...(#G-014)"`. 

APPROPRIATE: The main-thread guard correctly mirrors the source's own gate in src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:131, where the SIGTERM handler is only installed on the main thread — so the test asserts unconditionally exactly where the handler must exist, without over-asserting on worker-thread runners. The `else: pass` is honestly marked `# pragma: no cover`. Idiomatic, no regression: full tests/test_observability.py runs 23 passed / 2 skipped.

COMPLETE / DISCRIMINATES: I verified by reverting the SIGTERM wiring block in tracing.py (leaving only atexit.register). The test then FAILED with the exact assertion `AssertionError: SIGTERM flush handler not installed on the main thread (#G-014)` / `assert 'h' in {}`. After restoring the source, the test passes. pytest confirmed to run on the main thread (the guard is satisfied), so the discriminating assertion is actually exercised. The named regression test fails if the fix is reverted — it discriminates.

content.text: Verdict: CONFIRMED. The G-014 fix in tests/test_observability.py is both appropriate and complete — the self-gating `if "h" in captured:` was replaced with an unconditional main-thread assertion that mirrors the source's own main-thread gate, and I revert-tested it: dropping the SIGTERM wiring in tracing.py makes the named test fail with the exact `#G-014` assertion. No regression (23 passed / 2 skipped), source restored cleanly.
